## [00:00 - 03:03]

All right, everybody, it's now time to cover placement, bidding, and budget, which all happens at the ad set level of your Facebook campaign. So let's hop in and see what this looks like. All right, everybody. So in this video, we're going to talk about some things that happen at the ad set level that we didn't cover in the targeting modules. So things like placement, bidding, and budget. So let me just open this here for reference. A lot of what I'm talking about, and by the way, just to get through to this part, I said our objective as conversion. But most of what I'm talking about happens from here down. Now this area of ads manager will be different depending on which objective you choose. Now for placement, it's pretty much always going to be the same. And here's what I'll tell you about placements. I recommend running auto placements. So all placements. And in case you didn't know, placements are where are you going to show this ad on Facebook or Instagram, right? Where are we going to show it? So especially when I'm first starting out and testing, I like to use auto placements unless there's a reason I can't use a certain placement. For example, if you have a video over 60 seconds, it can't play on Instagram as an ad. So you would have to unselect that particular placement. So I always recommend running on auto placements. But just so you guys have a visual here of the different placements, we now have at our disposal. So they have them separated by platform. So first is feed. So of course that's desktop or mobile, your regular Facebook feed, instant articles. So and whenever you mouse over, you get a little display here, right? So these are the articles that show up and you click and they actually appear right inside of Facebook. In stream videos. So these are videos that are going to play in the middle of another video. We have right hand column. I think everyone knows what that is. Suggested videos are going to come up underneath another video. Ads can appear in video feeds across services, including suggested videos. Marketplace, so the Facebook marketplace and then Facebook stories. Instagram, we have a feed. We have stories. Audience network is basically third party media properties that have allowed Facebook to show ads on their properties. So this is always good to test. These can be done in video form too, as you can see here. And naturally just with our regular banner ads.

## [03:03 - 06:04]

And then messenger, right? So we have inbox messages. So this would be more when someone's scrolling through their inbox and looking at whose message them your ad would show up here. Stories, right? And then sponsored messages. So these are the messages that you send, but you pay to be promotional in. So I just wanted you guys to know all of these options are here. You're also able to choose what device. I normally do not choose a specific device. It normally does not matter to me. All right, so now scrolling down for budgets. What I do know about Facebook is that the bigger the budget that you give them, usually the better results you're going to get out of the gate. So you can start with as little as $5 a day, right? But I really recommend at least trying to spend $100 to $200 a day on a campaign to get some really good data quick and to give Facebook that budget that they need. I always use daily budget unless I'm in the middle of a launch or something that's really timely, right? That I know I'm going to have to shut off that ad eventually. Then I will use lifetime so that Facebook knows they only have a certain number of days to spend X amount of dollars. But again, I only recommend using that if you know you're going to shut that ad off. Otherwise, it can be hard to optimize after the time's run out if you want to keep going. You have the option to schedule a start and end date. And then as we scroll down here, optimization. So these are going to change depending on which objective you choose. And I included these in the last video. But usually I go with whatever Facebook has set for default here. Under conversion window, this is specific to the conversion objective. But I usually go for one day after clicking or viewing, right? So that Facebook can get a good amount of data, but that we're really wanting people to take that action within one day, right? If this is something like a purchase or something that's a little longer of a sales cycle, I might use the seven days. For bid strategy, I always go with lowest cost. Sometime you can use target cost and get lower CPAs to start. But then it just doesn't scale as well as it does when you use lowest cost. So I always go with Facebook's default on that. And the rest of this is pretty standard. You don't really have to make any selections down here. But definitely it's always best when in doubt to go with Facebook's default preferences in these areas. They do know best here. So it's just really important to think about your placements and making sure that your ad creatives are built for the placements that you're running ads on.
